subsystem: Linux kernel x86-64 eBPF JIT compiler core translation loop. `do_jit()` is the per-pass code generator that walks a verified eBPF instruction stream (`bpf_prog->insnsi`) and emits equivalent x86-64 machine code, first into a small per-instruction stack scratch buffer `temp[]` and then (when `image != NULL`) into the final executable JIT image. It handles ALU/ALU64, MOV, load-immediate (including 64-bit DW), DIV/MOD, MUL, shifts, byteswap, ST/STX/LDX (including probe-mem with an exception table), atomic RMW/CMPXCHG loops, direct/indirect calls, tail calls, conditional and unconditional jumps, and function exit.
provenance: Identified from unmistakable Linux-kernel BPF-JIT identifiers, not from any SPDX header (the banner is stripped). The signature `static int do_jit(struct bpf_prog *bpf_prog, int *addrs, u8 *image, int oldproglen, struct jit_context *ctx, bool jmp_padding)`, the `EMIT1/EMIT2/EMIT3/EMIT*_off32` emit macros, `__bpf_call_base`, `ex_handler_bpf`, `reg2pt_regs[]`, `simple_alu_opcodes[]`, `bpf_prog->aux->{tail_call_reachable,stack_depth,extable,num_exentries,poke_tab,func_idx}`, the BPF opcode class/op macros (`BPF_ALU`, `BPF_ALU64`, `BPF_JMP`, `BPF_LDX|BPF_PROBE_MEM`, `BPF_CLASS/BPF_OP/BPF_SIZE/BPF_SRC/BPF_MODE`), and raw x86 opcode bytes place this in `arch/x86/net/bpf_jit_comp.c` (GPL-2.0). The `sample:` comment and `train_0107_cwe77.c` filename are dataset bookkeeping, not evidence of the actual bug class, and were ignored.
trust_boundary: The eBPF program under compilation originates in user space via the bpf(2) syscall. Every field this loop reads from `insn` (`insn->code`, `insn->imm`, `insn->off`, `insn->dst_reg`, `insn->src_reg`, `insn[1].imm`) and the aggregate `bpf_prog->len`, `poke_tab[]`, `num_exentries`, `stack_depth` are ultimately attacker-influenced. They are, however, expected to have already passed the eBPF verifier: the JIT is a TRUSTING back end that assumes the verifier already bounded register numbers, jump targets, DW-imm pairing, and call destinations. The security contract is therefore split — any arithmetic the JIT performs that is NOT independently re-validated here relies entirely on those verifier invariants holding; if a verifier gap or an unprivileged-eBPF configuration lets a malformed but 'verified' program through, the resulting out-of-bounds happens in ring 0 while generating executable code, which is maximally severe.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point of one JIT pass. `bpf_prog` is the verified program being compiled. `addrs` is an int array indexed by BPF instruction number that records the cumulative x86 byte offset at which each BPF instruction ends — it is both written here and read to compute relative jump displacements. `image` is the destination executable buffer; on the sizing pass it is NULL (only lengths are computed), and on emitting passes it points at the allocated JIT image. `oldproglen` is the image size established by the previous pass and is used as the copy bound. `ctx` carries cross-pass state (`cleanup_addr`). `jmp_padding` selects whether jumps are padded to a fixed size so the image size converges across passes.

Local state. `insn` points at the first BPF instruction; `insn_cnt = bpf_prog->len` is the attacker-supplied (verified) instruction count that bounds the main loop. `callee_regs_used[4]` is a fixed 4-element flag array for the callee-saved registers, filled by `detect_reg_usage`. The critical buffer is `temp[BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE + BPF_INSN_SAFETY]`: a small fixed-size STACK scratch buffer into which each single BPF instruction's x86 expansion is emitted. `prog = temp` is the moving write cursor that the `EMIT*` macros advance; `ilen = prog - temp` later measures how many bytes were emitted for the current instruction. `proglen` accumulates the total emitted size; `excnt` counts exception-table entries produced.

A pre-scan over all `insn_cnt` instructions to determine which callee-saved registers the program uses (so the prologue/epilogue only save/restore what is needed) and whether any tail call is present. If a tail call is seen anywhere, `tail_call_reachable` is forced true, which changes prologue layout and how calls compute their stack access.

Emits the function prologue into `temp` via `prog`, sizing the stack frame from `bpf_prog->aux->stack_depth`, distinguishing classic (cBPF-origin) programs, threading through tail-call reachability, and flagging whether this is a bpf-to-bpf subprogram (`func_idx != 0`). It then pushes the callee-saved registers that `detect_reg_usage` marked. Both write into the same `temp` scratch buffer through the shared `prog` cursor.

The prologue is flushed: `ilen` is the number of prologue bytes emitted, and when compiling for real (`image` non-NULL) those bytes are copied from the stack scratch into `image + proglen`. `proglen` advances, `addrs[0]` records the end offset of the prologue block, and `prog` is reset to the start of `temp` for the next instruction. Note this flush has none of the per-instruction guards the main loop uses below.

The main translation loop iterates instructions 1..insn_cnt (index 0 is the prologue slot). Per instruction it snapshots the attacker-controlled fields: `imm32` (32-bit immediate, signed), `dst_reg`/`src_reg` (register numbers), and declares scratch for opcode bytes, a pointer to the start of an emitted ldx, a 64-bit jump offset, an x86 condition code, a call target pointer, and a nop count for jump padding.

The giant `switch (insn->code)` dispatches on the full BPF opcode. Register-to-register ALU/ALU64 arithmetic emits an optional REX/mod prefix (`maybe_emit_mod`) sized by whether it is 64-bit, looks up the x86 opcode byte from the `simple_alu_opcodes[]` table indexed by `BPF_OP(insn->code)`, and emits a 2-byte instruction encoding both registers via `add_2reg`. The table index derives from the opcode nibble; register numbers feed ModRM byte construction.

Register move (`MOV X`) is delegated to `emit_mov_reg` with a 64-bit flag. Negation emits a REX prefix (0x48 for 64-bit, or 0x40 when the register is an extended r8-r15 register per `is_ereg`), then the `F7 /3` NEG form via `add_1reg(0xD8, dst_reg)`.

what_the_code_does.code:
case BPF_ALU | BPF_ADD | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_SUB | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_AND | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_OR | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_XOR | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_ADD | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_SUB | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_AND | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_OR | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_XOR | BPF_K:
			if (BPF_CLASS(insn->code) == BPF_ALU64)
				EMIT1(add_1mod(0x48, dst_reg));
			else if (is_ereg(dst_reg))
				EMIT1(add_1mod(0x40, dst_reg));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Immediate-form ALU/ALU64 (opcode class `_K`). After the REX prefix, a nested switch on `BPF_OP` picks the x86 opcode byte pair (`b3` normal ModRM form, `b2` short EAX/RAX form). The immediate is then encoded in the smallest form: `is_imm8` uses the sign-extended 8-bit `0x83` form, `is_axreg` uses the accumulator short form, otherwise the full 32-bit `0x81` form — all emitting `imm32` directly into `temp`.

Load-immediate. The 32-bit MOV-K delegates to `emit_mov_imm32`. The 64-bit DW load is a two-slot BPF instruction: it reads the high half from `insn[1].imm` and the low half from `insn[0].imm`, then advances BOTH `insn++` and `i++` to skip the trailing slot. This is one of the few places the loop consumes a second instruction word, which makes the pairing an implicit precondition.

what_the_code_does.code:
/* dst %= src, dst /= src, dst %= imm32, dst /= imm32 */
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_MOD | BPF_X:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_DIV | BPF_X:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_MOD | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_DIV | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_MOD | BPF_X:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_DIV | BPF_X:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_MOD | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_DIV | BPF_K:
			EMIT1(0x50); /* push rax */
			EMIT1(0x52); /* push rdx */
what_the_code_does.explanation: Division/modulo. Because x86 DIV clobbers RAX and RDX, the sequence saves both, loads the divisor into the AUX register R11 (from `src_reg` or a 32-bit immediate), moves the dividend into RAX, zeroes RDX, performs 32- or 64-bit `div r11`, copies the quotient or remainder into R11, restores RDX/RAX, and finally moves R11 back into `dst_reg`. This is one of the longer emit sequences and thus the most bytes written into `temp` for a single instruction.

Multiplication similarly conditionally saves RAX/RDX (skipping the save when `dst_reg` already is that register), moves the multiplicand into R11, loads the multiplier into RAX (register or immediate), emits `mul r11`, then conditionally restores and moves the product from RAX into `dst_reg`. The conditional pushes/pops mean the emitted length depends on `dst_reg`, so the byte count varies per instruction.

what_the_code_does.code:
case BPF_ALU | BPF_LSH | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_RSH | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU | BPF_ARSH | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_LSH | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_RSH | BPF_K:
		case BPF_ALU64 | BPF_ARSH | BPF_K:
what_the_code_does.explanation: Shifts. Immediate shifts emit `D1` (shift-by-1) or `C1 imm8` forms with the x86 shift subcode from `simple_alu_opcodes[]`. Variable shifts (by `src_reg`) must place the count in RCX/CL: the code special-cases `dst_reg == BPF_REG_4` (which maps to RCX) by relocating it to R11 first, pushes/loads RCX, emits `D3`, restores RCX, and moves the R11 result back if needed. Register renaming here is delicate and register-number dependent.

Byte-swap / endianness conversion, dispatched on `imm32` being 16, 32, or 64 to emit the appropriate `ror`/`bswap`/`movzwl`/`mov` sequences for big-endian (`FROM_BE`) or little-endian (`FROM_LE`) normalization. `imm32` here is used as a width selector, not an arbitrary value; unlisted widths fall through emitting nothing.

Store-immediate to memory. The B/H/W/DW size cases emit the size-appropriate `MOV [mem], imm` opcode/prefix then fall through the shared `st:` label, which encodes the displacement `insn->off` as imm8 or off32 and emits the immediate sized by `bpf_size_to_x86_bytes(BPF_SIZE(insn->code))`. Register-to-memory stores (STX) at lines 372-378 delegate wholesale to `emit_stx`.

Memory loads. For safe `PROBE_MEM` loads (which may fault on a bad pointer), the code first emits a `test src_reg,src_reg` + `jne` guard, a zeroing of `dst_reg`, and a `jmp` over the load, then back-patches the two short jump displacements (`temp[4]` and `start_of_ldx[-1]`) once the real load length is known. It then registers an exception-table entry: it checks `excnt` against `bpf_prog->aux->num_exentries`, computes signed 32-bit relative deltas for `ex->insn` and `ex->handler` (rejecting if they do not fit), guards `dst_reg > BPF_REG_9`, and encodes the fixup as `(prog - temp) | (reg2pt_regs[dst_reg] << 8)`. These bounds checks are the model for what the rest of the loop assumes.

what_the_code_does.code:
case BPF_STX | BPF_ATOMIC | BPF_W:
		case BPF_STX | BPF_ATOMIC | BPF_DW:
			if (insn->imm == (BPF_AND | BPF_FETCH) ||
			    insn->imm == (BPF_OR | BPF_FETCH) ||
			    insn->imm == (BPF_XOR | BPF_FETCH)) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Atomic RMW. Fetch-and-bitop variants cannot be a single x86 instruction, so a CMPXCHG retry loop is emitted: save R0 to RAX, load the old value, compute the op into AUX_REG, attempt `cmpxchg`, and `jne` back to the loop start on failure (encoding a backward branch as `-(prog - branch_target) - 2`). Simpler atomics call `emit_atomic`. Both propagate an `err` return on generation failure.

Helper/bpf-to-bpf calls compute the absolute target as `__bpf_call_base + imm32`, where `imm32` is the verifier-encoded call offset. When tail calls are reachable, it first reloads the tail-call counter from the stack frame (`stack_depth + 8`). `emit_call` then emits a `call rel32` relative to the current image address `image + addrs[i-1]` (+7 to account for the reload). Tail calls (line 522) dispatch to `emit_bpf_tail_call_direct` using `poke_tab[imm32 - 1]` for known targets or `emit_bpf_tail_call_indirect` otherwise.

Conditional-jump lowering. After emitting the compare/test (register, immediate, or the zero-optimized `test dst,dst`), control reaches `emit_cond_jmp:`, which maps the BPF condition to an x86 condition code. The branch displacement is `jmp_offset = addrs[i + insn->off] - addrs[i]` (line 662): the difference between the recorded end offset of the target instruction and of the current one. If it fits imm8 it emits a 2-byte `Jcc rel8` (with optional nop padding under `jmp_padding` to keep the image size stable across passes); otherwise a 6-byte `0F 8x rel32`; anything larger is a generation bug and returns -EFAULT.

Unconditional jump. A `off == -1` self-loop is special-cased to `-2`; otherwise the offset is computed the same way as conditional jumps via `addrs[i + insn->off]`. A zero offset is optimized out (or padded with nops when `jmp_padding`). The `emit_jmp:` tail encodes `EB rel8` or `E9 rel32`. This shares the same `addrs[i + insn->off]` indexing contract as the conditional path.

Program exit. The first EXIT emits the real epilogue (restore callee regs, `leave`, `ret`) and records its address in `ctx->cleanup_addr`; every subsequent EXIT is compiled as a jump to that shared epilogue. The `default:` case (781-790) returns -EINVAL for any opcode the JIT does not recognize, catching verifier/JIT desync or junk in `bpf_prog`.

Per-instruction flush and the loop's two safety checks. `ilen` is the emitted byte count for this instruction; it is compared against `BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE` (the check is POST-emission — the bytes are already in `temp`). When compiling for real, `proglen + ilen` is compared against `oldproglen` before the `memcpy` into `image`, guarding the image copy. Then `proglen` advances, `addrs[i]` records this instruction's end offset (consumed by later jump-offset math), and `prog` resets to `temp`.

After the loop, a final consistency check ensures the number of exception-table entries emitted (`excnt`) exactly matches the pre-allocated `num_exentries`, catching under/over-population of the extable. On success the function returns the total emitted program length, which the caller uses to size or finalize the image.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: `temp` is a fixed-size stack buffer and every `EMIT*` in the switch writes into it through `prog` with NO bound check at the point of writing. The only size validation is post-hoc at line 793 (`if (ilen > BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE)`), which runs AFTER all bytes for the instruction are already in `temp`. The design relies on the invariant that no single opcode case can emit more than `BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE + BPF_INSN_SAFETY` bytes; the `BPF_INSN_SAFETY` slack exists precisely because the check is deferred. If any case (or a newly added opcode, or an unusual register/immediate combination in the long DIV/MOD or shift sequences) expands to more bytes than the buffer holds, the write smashes the kernel stack (CWE-787) before line 793 can reject it. The implementation must guarantee the worst-case emission of every switch arm is bounded by `sizeof(temp)`.

The final JIT image (`image`, a module_alloc/executable buffer) is written by `memcpy(image + proglen, temp, ilen)`. Its only bound is `proglen + ilen <= oldproglen`, where `oldproglen` is the size the PREVIOUS pass computed. This depends on the multi-pass JIT converging: if a later pass ever emits more bytes than the sizing pass did (e.g. a jump that should have shrunk grows instead, or padding math is wrong), `proglen` can exceed the allocation. This in-loop check catches that with -EFAULT — but the PROLOGUE copy at line 30 (`if (image) memcpy(image + proglen, temp, ilen);`) has NO such guard and no `ilen > BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE` check; it trusts entirely that the sizing pass reserved room for the prologue. A prologue that grows between passes (e.g. via `stack_depth`/tail-call layout changes) would overflow `image` on the very first copy (CWE-787). The invariant is strict image-size convergence across passes.

Both conditional (line 662) and unconditional (line 708) jumps index `addrs[i + insn->off]` with `insn->off`, an attacker-supplied 16-bit signed field. `addrs` is sized to `insn_cnt + 1`; the JIT does NOT re-check that `i + insn->off` lands in `[0, insn_cnt]`, trusting the verifier to have validated every jump target. If a malformed-but-'verified' program (verifier gap, or a config permitting it) carries an out-of-range `off`, this reads `addrs` out of bounds (CWE-125), yielding a garbage `jmp_offset`. A wrong-but-in-imm32-range offset is silently encoded, producing a branch to an attacker-chosen location inside/near the executable image — i.e. corrupted ring-0 control flow. This branch-displacement contract is exactly the class of the documented x86 BPF-JIT branch-displacement miscompilation. The implementation must ensure jump targets are bounded and that displacements are recomputed to convergence.

The 64-bit immediate load reads `insn[1].imm`, one `struct bpf_insn` past the current one, and advances both `insn` and `i`. This assumes every `BPF_LD|BPF_IMM|BPF_DW` occupies two consecutive slots and is never the final instruction. If a program's last instruction were a DW-load (a verifier invariant the JIT does not re-check), `insn[1]` reads one element beyond the `insnsi[]` array (CWE-125 out-of-bounds read), and `i++` then makes the loop condition `i <= insn_cnt` skip/miscount. The safety contract is verifier-enforced DW pairing and non-terminal placement.

The call target is `__bpf_call_base + imm32` with `imm32` straight from the instruction. The JIT does not itself validate that this resolves to a legitimate helper or bpf-subprogram entry — it relies on the verifier having rewritten/validated the call. If an unvalidated `imm32` reaches here, `emit_call` emits a `call` to an attacker-computed address (missing-validation, CWE-20), which on a JIT back end is effectively arbitrary kernel code execution. Additionally `emit_bpf_tail_call_direct(&bpf_prog->aux->poke_tab[imm32 - 1], ...)` at line 524 indexes `poke_tab[]` by `imm32 - 1`; the guarding `if (imm32)` only excludes zero, so the upper bound of `imm32` against the poke table size is another verifier-trusted invariant. The implementation must guarantee call `imm32` and tail-call `imm32` are validated/bounded before this point.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
temp[4] = prog - temp - 5 /* sizeof(test + jne) */;
				start_of_ldx = prog;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The PROBE_MEM path back-patches two SINGLE-BYTE relative jump displacements: `temp[4] = prog - temp - 5` and later `start_of_ldx[-1] = prog - start_of_ldx` (line 411). These 8-bit slots can only represent a signed offset in [-128,127]. The safety contract is that the emitted `ldx` body between the patch site and its target is always short enough to fit an 8-bit displacement. If `emit_ldx` ever produced a longer sequence (e.g. an unexpected size/register combination), the displacement would be truncated (CWE-190), silently mis-directing the fault-skip branch in the generated code so a faulting load would not be skipped correctly. In practice ldx encodings are small, so this is a low-severity contract, but it is a genuine assumption to confirm against the actual `emit_ldx` implementation.

summary: This is the x86-64 eBPF JIT translation loop (`do_jit`, arch/x86/net/bpf_jit_comp.c). It contains no self-evident overflow in the shown code, but it is a security-critical, verifier-trusting back end: it emits into a fixed stack buffer `temp[]` with only a POST-emission size check, copies into the executable `image` bounded solely by the previous pass's `oldproglen` (with the prologue copy at line 30 wholly unguarded), and performs jump/call/DW-load arithmetic (`addrs[i + insn->off]`, `__bpf_call_base + imm32`, `insn[1].imm`, `poke_tab[imm32-1]`) whose safety rests entirely on eBPF-verifier invariants it does not re-check. Any gap in those invariants turns into a ring-0 out-of-bounds write/read or branch-displacement miscompilation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: do_jit(bpf_prog, addrs, image, oldproglen, ctx, jmp_padding) signature (2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Params carry the trust boundary: addrs indexing, image bound (oldproglen), and verified-but-attacker-origin bpf_prog drive every downstream risk.
Fixed stack emit buffer written by EMIT* with only a post-hoc size check; central stack-overflow surface.
Loop bound only; used as `i <= insn_cnt`, no buffer is indexed directly by it here.
Fixed 4-element flag array written by detect_reg_usage/push/pop_callee_regs with a fixed index domain (4 callee regs); no attacker-controlled index.
Emit cursor and emitted-length; ilen feeds both the size check and the memcpy length.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: memcpy(image + proglen, temp, ilen) prologue copy (30)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Image copy with NO ilen or oldproglen guard, unlike the in-loop copy; relies entirely on sizing-pass reservation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: memcpy(image + proglen, temp, ilen) in-loop + proglen+ilen>oldproglen (799-803)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Image write bounded only by prior-pass oldproglen; correctness depends on multi-pass convergence.
Writes at bounded indices 0..insn_cnt into caller-sized addrs; the read side is the risk, not these writes.
OOB read / branch-displacement hazard indexed by attacker-supplied insn->off; verifier-trusted.
i starts at 1 so i-1 >= 0; in-bounds for calls; no attacker-controlled index arithmetic.
Attacker-origin fields used as immediates, table indices, and register numbers throughout; feed the call-target and shift/register-rename logic.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: emit_mov_imm64(&prog, dst_reg, insn[1].imm, insn[0].imm); insn++; i++ (138-140)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Reads insn[1] one slot ahead; OOB read if DW-load is terminal.
Indexed by the 4-bit BPF_OP nibble into a table sized for all ops; index domain is closed and not directly overflowable from the shown code.
Call target derived from raw imm32 without JIT-side validation; verifier-trusted arbitrary-call contract.
poke_tab indexed by imm32-1 with only a nonzero guard; upper bound is verifier-trusted (covered with the call finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: temp[4] = prog - temp - 5; start_of_ldx[-1] = prog - start_of_ldx (401, 411)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Single-byte jump-displacement back-patches; truncation hazard if the ldx sequence is long.
Bounds check is PRESENT and correct (rejects overflow before indexing); a protective guard, not a defect.
Table index is guarded immediately above by the dst_reg > BPF_REG_9 check returning -EFAULT; bounded.
Range checks that reject out-of-range displacements with -EFAULT; protective, not defects.
Present but POST-emission; the corruption (if any) already occurred in temp before this runs (covered by the temp finding).
Cross-pass epilogue address; a computed int offset, no buffer or attacker index.
Correct post-loop consistency guard; protective.
Safe default rejects unrecognized opcodes; protective.
Padding is range-validated (nops must be one of the expected values, else -EFAULT) and only affects convergence, folded into the image-size finding.

findings.title: Emit into fixed stack buffer temp[] is bounded only by a post-emission size check
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (unbounded emit before deferred size check)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0107_cwe77.c (and the EMIT* macros / emit_* helpers in the implementation, files not provided)
findings.invariant: The worst-case x86 expansion of EVERY switch arm (including the multi-step DIV/MOD, MUL, variable-shift, and PROBE_MEM sequences and any future opcode) must be <= sizeof(temp) = BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE + BPF_INSN_SAFETY, because the size check at line 793 runs only after all bytes are already written to temp.
findings.failure_mode: If any single instruction's emission exceeds the buffer, the EMIT* writes past temp on the kernel stack before line 793 can reject it; the check then reports -EFAULT on already-corrupted state. Trigger is an opcode/operand combination (or an added opcode) whose lowering exceeds the safety margin. Impact: ring-0 stack corruption during JIT.
findings.what_to_confirm: Sum the maximum bytes emitted by each case arm and each emit_* helper and confirm every path stays within BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE + BPF_INSN_SAFETY; confirm BPF_INSN_SAFETY covers the largest arm (DIV/MOD and variable shift).

findings.title: JIT image copy bounded only by previous-pass oldproglen; prologue copy is unguarded
findings.anchor: memcpy(image + proglen, temp, ilen) with proglen + ilen > oldproglen guard, and the unguarded prologue memcpy
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write into executable JIT image (multi-pass non-convergence)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0107_cwe77.c and the JIT image allocator/pass driver (bpf_int_jit_compile, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The image allocation (sized from a prior pass's returned proglen == oldproglen) must always be >= the bytes emitted by every subsequent pass; the multi-pass loop must converge so proglen never exceeds oldproglen. The prologue emitted at lines 23-30 must be included in that reserved size.
findings.failure_mode: If a later pass emits more bytes than the sizing pass (e.g. a jump that grows instead of shrinking, or a prologue that changes size due to stack_depth/tail-call layout), proglen exceeds the allocation. The in-loop memcpy (803) catches this with -EFAULT, but the PROLOGUE memcpy at line 30 has no ilen or oldproglen check and would overflow the executable image directly. Impact: heap/vmalloc OOB write into RX memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the pass driver proves convergence and that the initial image size explicitly reserves the maximum prologue+epilogue; confirm the line-30 copy can never exceed the allocation (e.g. prologue size is fixed before image allocation).

findings.title: Jump displacement indexes addrs[] by attacker-supplied insn->off without JIT-side bound
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read of addrs[] + branch-displacement miscompilation
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (eBPF verifier that bounds jump targets, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The verifier must guarantee i + insn->off lies within [0, insn_cnt] for every JMP/JMP32/JA, and the multi-pass JIT must recompute displacements until they stabilize so is_imm8/is_simm32 encode the true target distance.
findings.failure_mode: A malformed-but-'verified' program with an out-of-range insn->off makes addrs[i + insn->off] read outside the addrs array (OOB read), producing a garbage jmp_offset that is silently encoded as an in-range rel8/rel32 branch to an attacker-influenced location in the executable image, corrupting ring-0 control flow. This is the classic x86 BPF-JIT branch-displacement failure class.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the verifier bounds all jump offsets and that addrs is sized insn_cnt+1; confirm displacement convergence so no branch is encoded from a stale/incorrect offset.

findings.title: Call target computed from raw imm32 (and tail-call poke_tab[imm32-1]) trusts the verifier
findings.bug_class: Missing validation of a control-transfer target (arbitrary call)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (eBPF verifier call-target rewrite, file not provided)
findings.invariant: imm32 for BPF_CALL must have been validated/rewritten by the verifier to a legitimate helper or bpf-subprogram entry; for BPF_TAIL_CALL, imm32-1 must be a valid index into bpf_prog->aux->poke_tab (the only guard here is the nonzero `if (imm32)`).
findings.failure_mode: An unvalidated imm32 yields func = __bpf_call_base + imm32 pointing at an arbitrary address and emit_call emits a call to it, i.e. arbitrary kernel code execution; an unbounded tail-call imm32 indexes poke_tab out of bounds. Both are only safe because the verifier is trusted upstream.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the verifier validates BPF_CALL imm32 destinations and bounds BPF_TAIL_CALL imm32 against the poke_tab size before the JIT runs.

findings.title: 64-bit immediate load reads insn[1] one slot beyond the current instruction
findings.anchor: emit_mov_imm64(&prog, dst_reg, insn[1].imm, insn[0].imm); insn++; i++;
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read of the instruction stream
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (eBPF verifier DW-imm pairing, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every BPF_LD|BPF_IMM|BPF_DW must be followed by a second instruction slot and must never be the final instruction, so insn[1] is always in-bounds of bpf_prog->insnsi[].
findings.failure_mode: If a DW-load is the last instruction (unpaired), insn[1].imm reads one struct bpf_insn past the insnsi[] array (OOB read), and i++ desynchronizes the loop counter against insn_cnt. Only the verifier's pairing invariant prevents this; the JIT does not re-check it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the verifier rejects a terminal or unpaired BPF_LD|BPF_IMM|BPF_DW so insn[1] is guaranteed present.

findings.title: PROBE_MEM fault-skip branches are back-patched into single-byte displacements
findings.anchor: temp[4] = prog - temp - 5; start_of_ldx[-1] = prog - start_of_ldx;
findings.bug_class: Integer truncation of an 8-bit relative jump displacement
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0107_cwe77.c and emit_ldx (implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The emitted ldx body between each patch site and its branch target must be short enough (<= 127 bytes) that the single-byte relative displacement written to temp[4] and start_of_ldx[-1] is not truncated.
findings.failure_mode: If emit_ldx ever emits a sequence long enough that prog - start_of_ldx (or prog - temp - 5) exceeds signed 8-bit range, the displacement stored in one byte is truncated, silently mis-directing the fault-skip/zeroing branch in the generated code so a faulting PROBE_MEM load is not handled correctly. Low severity because ldx encodings are small in practice, but it is an unchecked assumption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the maximum length of the emit_ldx output plus the surrounding test/jne/jmp bytes always fits an 8-bit displacement for every size/register combination.

audit_checklist: Prove that every switch arm and emit_* helper emits at most BPF_MAX_INSN_SIZE + BPF_INSN_SAFETY bytes into temp[], since the size check at line 793 is post-emission.

Verify the image allocation reserves space for the prologue/epilogue and that the multi-pass JIT provably converges so proglen never exceeds oldproglen; note the line-30 prologue memcpy has no runtime bound.
Confirm the verifier bounds every jump target so addrs[i + insn->off] (lines 662, 708) is always in [0, insn_cnt], and that branch displacements are recomputed to a fixed point.
Confirm the verifier validates BPF_CALL imm32 destinations and bounds BPF_TAIL_CALL imm32 against bpf_prog->aux->poke_tab before do_jit runs.
Confirm BPF_LD|BPF_IMM|BPF_DW is always paired and never terminal so insn[1].imm (line 138) cannot read past insnsi[].
Check that emit_ldx output length keeps the single-byte PROBE_MEM back-patch displacements (temp[4], start_of_ldx[-1]) within signed 8-bit range.
Confirm reg2pt_regs[dst_reg] (line 448) is only reached after the dst_reg > BPF_REG_9 guard and that register numbers used as ModRM/table inputs are all verifier-bounded.
Confirm the extable excnt accounting (lines 416-420, 810-813) exactly matches num_exentries on every pass and cannot under/over-run bpf_prog->aux->extable.
